policy_statement: Valorise genetic material of agricultural and forestry varieties and species to reduce susceptibility to emerging diseases and pests.
mechanism_description: Improving the genetic resilience of crops and forest species reduces vulnerability to emerging pests and diseases, strengthening climate adaptation capacity in managed and natural landscapes.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate stressors + shifting pest/disease dynamics → (genetic improvement/selection) → ↓ host susceptibility/↑ resistance → ↓ pest/disease damage → ↓ climate impact on yields/forest condition (↓ vulnerability)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Farmers/foresters and public agencies adopt, breed, or prioritize crop and tree varieties/species with genetic traits that confer resistance/tolerance to current and emerging pests and pathogens.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved host genetics reduces infection probability, slows pathogen replication/spread, increases plant defense and recovery, and maintains stand/crop vigor under climate stress, which otherwise increases pest/pathogen pressure.  
(3) Outcome: Reduced losses and dieback, more stable production and ecosystem services, and improved resilience of managed landscapes to climate-amplified biological threats (an adaptation benefit via reduced sensitivity/impact).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced climate-driven yield/forest-loss impacts by increasing resistance/tolerance to pests and diseases, thereby lowering vulnerability and damage under climate change.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism targets climate risk (pest/disease outbreaks intensified by climate change) by reducing exposure/sensitivity of crops/forests through genetic resilience—an impact-reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Any mitigation effects (e.g., avoided land-use change from avoided crop failure, reduced pesticide use) are indirect, context-dependent, and not the main or guaranteed outcome of breeding for resistance; the direct, intended, and most reliable effect is reduced climate damage.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—no secondary category is physically unavoidable across all instances (e.g., pesticide reduction or carbon benefits may or may not occur depending on management, baseline, and adoption).  
4) Edge cases applied: Even when resilient varieties increase productivity, that does not inherently translate to net GHG reductions; likewise, intensification effects could offset. Therefore, mitigation/resource-efficiency labels are not universally valid for the mechanism.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy implementing climate-resilient breeding for pest/disease resistance is fundamentally increasing system resilience to climate-amplified biological stressors, fitting Adaptation in all locations.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations can yield mitigation co-benefits (e.g., avoided deforestation from stabilized yields, reduced pesticide/fuel use), but these are not universal and can be offset by rebound/intensification; classification remains Adaptation.
key_indicators: “Valorisation of the genetic material”; “agricultural and forestry varieties and species”; “reduce susceptibility”; “emerging diseases and pests”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved yield stability and forest health; possible reduced pesticide use; potential indirect support for drought/heat resilience if co-selected traits exist (not stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument is not explicitly specified (could be R&D funding, certification, incentives, or procurement); classified as a planning/strategy-type statement because it sets a direction (“valorisation…to reduce susceptibility”) rather than naming a concrete mandate or spending program.
